explanation: CSS transitions animate a property change from one value to another smoothly. CSS animations use @keyframes to define multi-step sequences. For performance, you should only animate properties that trigger compositing — primarily `transform` and `opacity`. Animating `width`, `height`, `top`, or `left` triggers layout recalculation (reflow) on every frame, consuming significant CPU. `transform` and `opacity` changes are handled by the GPU compositor thread without touching the layout engine, achieving silky 60fps even on low-end devices. Use `will-change: transform` sparingly to hint the browser to promote an element to its own GPU layer.
strongAnswerPoints: Only transform and opacity are compositor-only — no reflow, handled by GPU.
Animating width/height/top/left triggers layout recalculation every frame.
will-change promotes element to GPU layer — use sparingly, not on every element.
whatToAvoid: Don't add will-change: transform to everything — too many GPU layers exhaust VRAM on mobile devices.
ytQuery: CSS animations performance transform opacity GPU
quickAnswer: CSS transitions and @keyframe animations are smooth when using transform and opacity — these are GPU-composited and never trigger layout. Animating geometric properties (width, height, position) causes reflow every frame, dropping frames and killing performance. Keep animations on transform/opacity and use will-change sparingly.
eli10: Imagine rearranging furniture every second vs just changing the room's lighting. Rearranging furniture (layout properties) is exhausting and slow. Changing the lights (transform/opacity) is instant. GPU animations are the lighting — fast with no effort.
realWorldExample: Airbnb's date picker originally animated width and height changes for calendar transitions, causing 30fps jank on mobile. Rewriting it to use transform: scaleX() and opacity transitions brought it to a consistent 60fps with no layout calculations.

question: What are CSS Pseudo-classes and Pseudo-elements? Give practical examples.
summary: Distinguishes state-based selectors from content-inserting selectors and their respective use cases.
whatTheyTest: CSS selector knowledge and ability to style component states and decorative content without extra HTML.
explanation: Pseudo-classes select elements based on their state or position: `:hover`, `:focus`, `:active`, `:visited`, `:nth-child()`, `:not()`, `:is()`, `:where()`. They use a single colon. Pseudo-elements create or target a sub-part of an element: `::before` and `::after` insert decorative content before/after the element's content; `::placeholder` styles placeholder text; `::first-line` targets the first rendered line. They use double colons (though browsers accept single colon for legacy reasons). Pseudo-elements require the `content` property to render (can be empty string).
strongAnswerPoints: Pseudo-classes (:hover, :focus): target element based on state — single colon.
Pseudo-elements (::before, ::after): create virtual child elements — double colon.
::before and ::after require content property; commonly used for decorative icons and the clearfix hack.
whatToAvoid: Don't use ::before/::after for meaningful content — they're invisible to screen readers.
ytQuery: CSS pseudo-class vs pseudo-element explained
quickAnswer: Pseudo-classes (single colon) select elements by state or position — :hover, :focus, :nth-child(). Pseudo-elements (double colon) create virtual nodes or target sub-parts — ::before, ::after, ::placeholder. The ::before/::after pseudo-elements require a content property and are invisible to screen readers, so use them only for decorative purposes.
eli10: Pseudo-classes are like moods for your elements — the button is 'in hover mood' so it changes color. Pseudo-elements are like adding invisible helpers — ::before puts a little helper in front of your text and ::after puts one behind it.
realWorldExample: GitHub uses ::before and ::after extensively for their icon bullets in markdown-rendered content and the activity timeline connector lines. This avoids adding extra DOM nodes for purely decorative elements, keeping the DOM lean.

question: What is JavaScript currying and what are its practical use cases?
summary: Explains transforming multi-argument functions into chains of single-argument functions.
whatTheyTest: Functional programming knowledge, partial application, and composable code design.
explanation: Currying transforms a function that takes multiple arguments into a sequence of functions each taking one argument. `add(2, 3)` becomes `add(2)(3)`. This enables partial application — calling a curried function with fewer arguments returns a new function with those arguments pre-filled. Practical uses: creating configurable event handlers, building composable middleware pipelines, and making function factories. Currying combined with function composition is the foundation of functional programming in JavaScript.
strongAnswerPoints: Currying transforms f(a, b) into f(a)(b) — each call takes one argument.
Partial application pre-fills some arguments, returning a specialized function.
Used in functional pipelines, event handler factories, and middleware composition.
whatToAvoid: Don't curry every function — it adds indirection and hurts readability when overused.
ytQuery: JavaScript currying partial application explained
quickAnswer: Currying transforms a multi-argument function into nested single-argument functions. f(a, b) becomes f(a)(b). Partial application pre-fills some arguments to create specialized functions. Common uses: configurable function factories, Redux reducer composition, and functional middleware pipelines.
eli10: Currying is like ordering a pizza by choosing one thing at a time. First you choose the size (medium), that gives you a 'medium pizza builder'. Then you choose the topping (cheese). You made two smaller decisions instead of one big one, and you can reuse the 'medium pizza builder' for different toppings.
realWorldExample: Ramda, a popular functional programming library for JavaScript, is built entirely around auto-curried functions. React's connect() HOC from react-redux is a curried function — connect(mapStateToProps)(Component) — allowing mapStateToProps to be reused across multiple components.

question: What are WeakMap and WeakSet in JavaScript and when should you use them?
summary: Explains weakly-held object references, garbage collection implications, and private data patterns.
whatTheyTest: Memory management knowledge, garbage collection, and advanced ES6 collection types.
explanation: Regular Map and Set hold strong references to their keys — the referenced objects cannot be garbage collected as long as the Map/Set exists. WeakMap and WeakSet hold weak references: if the only remaining reference to an object is in a WeakMap, the garbage collector can reclaim that object and automatically remove the entry. WeakMaps only allow object keys (not primitives) and are non-iterable. They're ideal for storing private metadata about DOM nodes or external objects — when the object is removed, its associated data is automatically cleaned up.
strongAnswerPoints: WeakMap keys must be objects — they're held weakly and can be GC'd automatically.
WeakMap is non-enumerable/non-iterable — you can't loop through entries.
Use for: caching DOM node metadata, private class data, preventing memory leaks in observers.
whatToAvoid: Don't use WeakMap as a regular cache — you can't check its size or clear it, and you can't iterate entries.
ytQuery: JavaScript WeakMap WeakSet explained garbage collection
quickAnswer: WeakMap and WeakSet hold weak references to objects — if no other reference to a key exists, the garbage collector removes it and the map entry automatically. Use WeakMap to cache metadata for DOM nodes or external objects without causing memory leaks. They're non-iterable and only accept object keys.
eli10: A WeakMap is like writing a note on someone's hand instead of in a book. If that person leaves the room (gets garbage collected), the note disappears automatically. A regular Map is like a book where the note stays forever even after the person leaves.
realWorldExample: React's internal Fiber architecture uses WeakMap-like structures to associate DOM nodes with their fiber metadata. When a DOM node is removed and has no references, its fiber data is automatically eligible for garbage collection, preventing the memory leaks that plagued earlier React versions.

question: What is the JavaScript Proxy object and what are its use cases?
summary: Covers intercepting fundamental object operations with get, set, and has traps for meta-programming.
whatTheyTest: Advanced ES6 meta-programming, reactive system internals, and API design patterns.
explanation: A Proxy wraps an object and intercepts fundamental operations via handler functions called 'traps'. The `get` trap fires when a property is read, `set` when a property is written, `has` when the `in` operator is used, `deleteProperty` for delete, and more. This is how reactive frameworks like Vue 3 implement their reactivity system — every data object is wrapped in a Proxy that notifies the renderer when properties change. Other uses: validation, logging, auto-defaulting missing properties, and mocking APIs in tests.
strongAnswerPoints: Proxy intercepts: get, set, deleteProperty, has, apply (for functions), and construct.
Vue 3's reactivity system wraps state objects in Proxies to track dependencies and trigger renders.
Use for: validation, default values, logging, sandboxing, and building observable objects.
whatToAvoid: Proxies add overhead on every property access — don't use them in tight performance-critical loops.
ytQuery: JavaScript Proxy object explained use cases
quickAnswer: Proxy wraps an object and intercepts operations via handler traps (get, set, has, deleteProperty, etc.). It enables meta-programming: Vue 3's reactivity, validation wrappers, auto-default objects, and API mocking. The Reflect API complements Proxy by providing default trap implementations.
eli10: A Proxy is like a secretary who sits in front of your boss's office. Every time someone tries to talk to the boss (access a property), they go through the secretary first. The secretary can check credentials, say 'no', log the visit, or even pretend the boss said something different.
realWorldExample: Vue 3 replaced its Object.defineProperty-based reactivity (Vue 2) with Proxy in Vue 3. The Proxy approach intercepts nested property access automatically, fixing Vue 2's inability to detect array mutation methods and new property additions, while reducing the reactivity system code by 40%.

question: How do WebSockets work and how do they differ from Server-Sent Events (SSE)?
summary: Compares full-duplex WebSocket communication with one-directional SSE streaming.
whatTheyTest: Real-time communication architecture and choosing the right protocol for different use cases.
explanation: WebSockets establish a persistent, full-duplex TCP connection between client and server — both sides can send messages at any time. They start as an HTTP upgrade request, then switch protocols. SSE (Server-Sent Events) is one-directional: the server pushes events to the client over a long-lived HTTP connection, but the client can only send data via regular HTTP requests. WebSockets suit true bidirectional apps (chat, multiplayer games, collaborative editing). SSE suits server-push scenarios (live notifications, live dashboards, streaming LLM responses) and works natively over HTTP/2 with auto-reconnect.
strongAnswerPoints: WebSocket: full-duplex, binary/text, requires WS server, lower overhead per message.
SSE: server-push only, HTTP-based, auto-reconnects, simpler infrastructure.
SSE is better for notifications/feeds; WebSocket is better for chat/games/collaborative editing.
whatToAvoid: Don't use WebSockets for simple notification use cases — SSE is simpler, works over HTTP/2, and auto-reconnects.
ytQuery: WebSockets vs Server-Sent Events SSE comparison
quickAnswer: WebSockets provide full-duplex communication — both client and server can send messages at any time. SSE is server-to-client only over a persistent HTTP connection. Use WebSockets for chat, multiplayer, collaborative editing. Use SSE for live feeds, dashboards, and streaming responses (like ChatGPT's token-by-token output) — SSE is simpler and natively HTTP-compatible.
eli10: WebSockets are like a phone call — both people can talk at any time. SSE is like a radio broadcast — the station talks, and you listen, but you can't talk back on the same channel. If you want to request a song, you call a separate phone number (HTTP POST).
realWorldExample: Vercel's AI SDK uses SSE (Server-Sent Events) to stream LLM token responses to the browser. As each token is generated server-side, it's pushed to the client immediately via SSE, giving the word-by-word appearance of ChatGPT responses without the complexity of a full WebSocket server.

question: What is CSS responsive design and how do media queries work?
summary: Covers mobile-first design strategy, breakpoints, and viewport meta tag setup.
whatTheyTest: Mobile-first CSS approach, breakpoint planning, and fluid layout techniques.
explanation: Responsive design means the layout adapts to different screen sizes. Media queries apply CSS rules conditionally based on viewport width, device orientation, or pixel density. Mobile-first approach writes base styles for small screens and uses `min-width` queries to progressively add styles for larger screens — this is better than desktop-first (`max-width`) because mobile is the default and additional styles layer on top. The viewport meta tag `<meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1'>` is required to prevent mobile browsers from zooming out and showing the desktop layout.
strongAnswerPoints: Mobile-first (min-width) is preferred — less CSS overriding and better performance on mobile.
The viewport meta tag is mandatory for responsive behavior on mobile browsers.
Use relative units (%, rem, vw/vh) instead of fixed px for fluid layouts.
whatToAvoid: Don't use only pixel-based breakpoints — different devices have different pixel densities; test in device mode.
ytQuery: CSS responsive design media queries mobile first
quickAnswer: Media queries apply CSS conditionally based on viewport dimensions. Mobile-first approach writes mobile base styles and layers desktop styles with min-width queries — the reverse (desktop-first with max-width) requires overriding styles on mobile. Always include the viewport meta tag, and use relative units (rem, %, vw) for fluid sizing.
eli10: Responsive design is like a chameleon that changes colors. When the screen is small (phone), it shows one column. When the screen is medium (tablet), two columns. When it's big (desktop), three columns. Media queries tell the website 'if the screen is wider than X pixels, use this layout instead.'
realWorldExample: Zomato's web interface uses mobile-first CSS with three breakpoints: 480px (mobile landscape), 768px (tablet), and 1200px (desktop). Their food listing grid goes from 1 column to 2 columns to 4 columns across these breakpoints, built with CSS Grid and min-width media queries.

question: What are ARIA roles and how do you make a custom component keyboard-accessible?
summary: Covers ARIA attributes, keyboard event handling, and focus management for custom interactive widgets.
whatTheyTest: Web accessibility standards, screen reader compatibility, and inclusive design implementation.
explanation: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes add semantic meaning to custom components that native HTML doesn't provide. Key attributes: `role` (what the element is — button, dialog, listbox), `aria-label` / `aria-labelledby` (accessible name), `aria-expanded` / `aria-checked` (state), and `aria-hidden` (exclude from accessibility tree). Keyboard accessibility requires: ensuring the element is focusable (tabIndex='0'), responding to keyboard events (Enter/Space for buttons, Arrow keys for lists), managing focus order with tabIndex, and trapping focus inside modals.
strongAnswerPoints: Use native HTML elements first (<button>, <input>) — they come with accessibility built in.
ARIA attributes communicate state and role to assistive technologies.
Keyboard access: focusable (tabIndex), keyboard events (Enter/Space), Escape to close, Arrow keys for navigation.
whatToAvoid: Don't add role='button' to a div and call it done — you must also add tabIndex, keyboard handlers, and correct ARIA states.
ytQuery: ARIA roles keyboard accessibility React
quickAnswer: ARIA attributes add semantic meaning to custom elements for screen readers: role defines what it is, aria-label names it, aria-expanded/checked communicate state. For keyboard access: add tabIndex='0' to make it focusable, handle Enter/Space as click, Arrow keys for navigation, Escape to close, and manage focus order. Prefer native HTML elements whenever possible.
eli10: ARIA is like adding labels in braille to things that don't have them. A custom dropdown made of divs doesn't tell a blind person's screen reader what it is. ARIA says 'this is a button that opens a list' so the screen reader can announce it correctly.
realWorldExample: GitHub's issue label picker is a custom multi-select dropdown built entirely from divs. They implemented full ARIA role='listbox' with aria-multiselectable, Arrow key navigation between aria-option elements, and focus management so keyboard-only and screen-reader users can apply labels without using a mouse.

question: How does async/await work under the hood and how does error handling differ from Promises?
summary: Explains async/await as syntactic sugar over Promises and try/catch vs .catch() patterns.
whatTheyTest: Deep understanding of async control flow, error propagation, and sequential vs parallel execution.
explanation: async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises. An `async` function always returns a Promise. `await` pauses execution of that function until the Promise resolves, then returns the resolved value. Under the hood, the JavaScript engine converts async/await into a generator-based state machine. Error handling uses try/catch with await, equivalent to `.catch()` on a Promise chain. A critical mistake: `await`ing in a loop runs requests sequentially. Use `Promise.all()` with mapped Promises to run requests in parallel.
strongAnswerPoints: async functions return a Promise — await unwraps it, pausing the function only (not the thread).
await in a loop is sequential — use Promise.all for parallel fetching.
Unhandled rejections in async functions propagate up — wrap top-level calls in try/catch.
whatToAvoid: Don't forget to await — calling an async function without await returns a pending Promise and the error is swallowed silently.
ytQuery: async await under the hood JavaScript Promises
quickAnswer: async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises and generators. await pauses the async function (not the event loop) until a Promise settles. Error handling uses try/catch instead of .catch(). Always use Promise.all when fetching multiple independent resources in parallel — await in a loop serializes them and multiplies wait time.
eli10: async/await is like telling your friend 'I'll wait here while you get the pizza' (await). Instead of doing other stuff and juggling a callback, you just wait at the door. Promise.all is like sending 5 friends to get different toppings at the same time — all go at once, then you put the pizza together.
realWorldExample: At Flipkart, a product detail page originally loaded reviews, seller info, and related products sequentially with three consecutive awaits, taking 1500ms total (3 x 500ms). Replacing with Promise.all reduced it to 500ms — the time of the slowest single request.

question: What is tree shaking and how does it reduce JavaScript bundle size?
summary: Explains dead code elimination via ES module static analysis during the bundling step.
whatTheyTest: Bundler knowledge, module systems, and techniques for reducing initial page load.
explanation: Tree shaking is dead code elimination performed by bundlers (Webpack, Vite, Rollup) during the build process. It's possible only with ES Modules (import/export) because their imports and exports are statically analyzable — the bundler can determine at build time exactly which exports are used. CommonJS (require/module.exports) is dynamic and cannot be tree-shaken reliably. When you import only one function from a utility library, the bundler includes only that function's code and removes all unused exports. Named imports enable better tree shaking than default imports.
strongAnswerPoints: Requires ES Modules (static import/export) — CommonJS require() cannot be tree-shaken.
Side-effect-free packages (sideEffects: false in package.json) are fully eligible.
Named imports are more tree-shakeable than importing a full default object.
whatToAvoid: Don't import entire libraries for one function — use named imports or per-method imports (lodash/debounce).
ytQuery: Tree shaking JavaScript bundle size Webpack Vite
quickAnswer: Tree shaking removes unused exports during bundling by statically analyzing ES Module imports. It requires ES Modules — CommonJS require() is dynamic and can't be analyzed at build time. Import only what you need via named imports, and ensure packages mark themselves as side-effect-free in package.json for maximum elimination.
eli10: Tree shaking is like a smart packer. You have a huge box of LEGO pieces (a library) but only need the blue ones (one function). Tree shaking removes all the other colors before shipping, so the box you ship is tiny instead of massive.
realWorldExample: An engineering team at CRED reduced their bundle from 2.1MB to 890KB by switching from lodash to lodash-es (ES module version) and auditing imports. Tree shaking eliminated 800KB of unused utility functions that the CommonJS version had included unconditionally.

question: What is the difference between reflow and repaint in browser rendering?
summary: Distinguishes layout recalculation from pixel redrawing and their performance costs.
whatTheyTest: Browser rendering pipeline knowledge and ability to diagnose performance bottlenecks.
explanation: Reflow (also called layout) occurs when the browser recalculates the geometry of elements — their size, position, and relationship to surrounding elements. It's triggered by changes to width, height, padding, margin, font size, or DOM structure. Repaint occurs when visual styles change without affecting layout — background color, outline, border color, box-shadow. Reflow is expensive because it can cascade: changing the width of a parent forces all children to recalculate. Composite-only changes (transform, opacity) skip both reflow and repaint, going straight to the compositor.
strongAnswerPoints: Reflow recalculates geometry — triggered by any layout property change. Expensive, cascades.
Repaint redraws pixels — triggered by visual-only changes. Cheaper than reflow.
Composite-only (transform, opacity) skips both — GPU handles it without touching layout.
whatToAvoid: Don't alternate reading and writing layout properties in a loop — this forces the browser to flush the layout queue on every read, causing forced synchronous layouts.
ytQuery: Browser reflow repaint compositing performance
quickAnswer: Reflow recalculates element geometry and position — triggered by layout property changes (width, height, padding). It cascades to children and is expensive. Repaint redraws visual appearance without geometry changes — cheaper. Compositing (transform, opacity) skips both — the GPU compositor handles it. Batch DOM reads before writes to avoid forced synchronous layouts.
eli10: Reflow is like rearranging all the furniture in your house — time-consuming because everything has to move. Repaint is like repainting the walls — you don't move furniture, just change colors. Compositing is like changing the wallpaper on your phone — so fast you don't even notice.
realWorldExample: Google's Web Fundamentals team documented the 'layout thrashing' bug in early Gmail. The inbox list alternated between reading offsetHeight and setting style.height for every row, triggering thousands of forced synchronous layouts per render. Caching offsetHeight before the loop brought render time from 1400ms to 65ms.

question: What is React Suspense and how does it work with lazy loading and data fetching?
summary: Explains the Suspense boundary as a loading coordinator for code splitting and async data.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of React concurrent features, data fetching patterns, and progressive loading UI.
explanation: Suspense is a React mechanism for declaratively specifying loading states. A Suspense boundary wraps components that may not be ready to render yet, displaying the fallback UI until all suspended children resolve. It currently has two confirmed use cases: (1) Lazy loading with React.lazy — Suspense shows the fallback while the chunk loads; (2) Data fetching — frameworks like Next.js 13+ and React Query 5 integrate with Suspense so components that await data suspend until the data arrives. Suspense removes the need for manual `isLoading` checks inside components.
strongAnswerPoints: Suspense boundaries show fallback while children are suspended (loading).
React.lazy + Suspense is the standard pattern for code splitting.
Suspense for data fetching requires library support — Next.js, React Query 5, and Relay integrate with it.
whatToAvoid: Don't use Suspense for loading states you control with useState — it's designed for promise-based async that React can detect.
ytQuery: React Suspense lazy loading data fetching
quickAnswer: Suspense shows a fallback UI while children are loading (suspended). It works today with React.lazy for code splitting, and with Suspense-compatible data fetching libraries (React Query 5, Next.js). It removes manual isLoading checks from components — the loading boundary is declared in the parent tree.
eli10: Suspense is like a loading screen placeholder. While the game (component) is loading, you see the loading screen (fallback). Once everything is ready, the loading screen disappears and the game starts. You don't have to code the 'if loading, show spinner' logic inside every component.
realWorldExample: Next.js 13's App Router uses Suspense natively. Every page's loading.tsx file is a Suspense fallback — when navigating to /dashboard, users see the loading skeleton from loading.tsx while the server component streams in. No manual loading state management required anywhere in the route.

question: What is memoization and how do you implement it in JavaScript?
summary: Covers caching function return values keyed to arguments to avoid repeated expensive computation.
whatTheyTest: Optimization techniques, closure usage, and understanding cache trade-offs.
explanation: Memoization is an optimization technique where a function caches its return value keyed to its input arguments. On subsequent calls with the same arguments, it returns the cached result instead of recomputing. This is useful for pure functions (same input always produces same output) that are computationally expensive — like Fibonacci, complex sorting, or API response transformations. The cache is typically a Map or plain object stored in a closure. useMemo and useCallback in React are React-specific memoization hooks.
strongAnswerPoints: Memoization only works correctly for pure functions — side-effectful functions produce incorrect cached results.
The cache key must uniquely represent the arguments — JSON.stringify for simple args, custom serializers for complex objects.
Memory trade-off: unbounded caches can grow indefinitely — use WeakMap or LRU for long-running apps.
whatToAvoid: Don't memoize functions with side effects or those that depend on external state — the cached result will be stale.
ytQuery: Memoization JavaScript implementation tutorial
quickAnswer: Memoization caches a function's return value keyed to its arguments. On repeat calls with identical arguments, it returns the cached value instantly. Implement with a Map inside a closure. Only works for pure functions. For React, useMemo caches computed values and useCallback caches function references across renders.
eli10: Memoization is like a math student who writes answers to hard problems on a cheat sheet. The first time they see '5 squared', they calculate 25 and write it down. Next time the same problem appears, they just look at the cheat sheet instead of solving it again.
realWorldExample: Reselect, the library used by Redux for creating selectors, uses memoization to prevent expensive state derivations from rerunning on every render. A selector that filters thousands of products by category only recomputes when the products array or category filter actually changes, not on every unrelated state update.
